name: The Human Brain
description: A tour of the brain's anatomy: the four lobes and what they do, the cerebellum and brainstem, the thalamus and hypothalamus, the limbic system, and the membranes and fluid that protect it all.

fields.front: What does the frontal lobe do?
fields.back: Voluntary movement, planning, decision-making, and personality
fields.extra: It holds the primary motor cortex and Broca's area, and is the last brain region to fully mature (into the mid-twenties).

fields.front: What does the parietal lobe do?
fields.back: Processes touch, temperature, pain, and spatial awareness
fields.extra: It contains the primary somatosensory cortex; damage can make a person ignore one whole side of space.

fields.front: What does the temporal lobe do?
fields.extra: It holds the auditory cortex and Wernicke's area, and the hippocampus lies deep inside it.

fields.front: What does the occipital lobe do?
fields.extra: The primary visual cortex sits at the very back of the brain, farthest from the eyes.

fields.front: What does Broca's area do, and where is it?
fields.back: It produces speech; it sits in the frontal lobe
fields.extra: Damage causes Broca's aphasia: speech becomes slow and effortful, but understanding stays intact.

fields.front: What does Wernicke's area do, and where is it?
fields.back: It handles language comprehension; it sits in the temporal lobe
fields.extra: Damage causes Wernicke's aphasia: speech stays fluent but makes little sense, and comprehension is poor.

fields.front: What is the largest part of the brain, seat of thought and voluntary action?
fields.extra: Split into two hemispheres and four lobes; its folded outer layer is the cerebral cortex.

fields.front: Which part of the brain coordinates movement, balance, and posture?
fields.extra: The 'little brain' at the lower back; it holds more than half of all the brain's neurons. Damage makes movement clumsy (ataxia).

fields.front: What are the three parts of the brainstem, from top to bottom?
fields.back: The midbrain, the pons, and the medulla oblongata
fields.extra: It links the brain to the spinal cord and runs many automatic body functions.

fields.front: Which part of the brainstem controls breathing, heart rate, and blood pressure?
fields.extra: It also drives reflexes like coughing, sneezing, swallowing, and vomiting. Damage here can be fatal.

fields.front: What does the pons do?
fields.back: Bridges the cerebrum and cerebellum and helps control breathing
fields.extra: Its name means 'bridge'; it also carries the nuclei of several cranial nerves.

fields.front: What does the midbrain help control?
fields.back: Eye movement and reflex responses to sights and sounds
fields.extra: The top part of the brainstem; it also helps regulate alertness.

fields.front: Which structure is the brain's main sensory relay, routing signals up to the cortex?
fields.extra: Almost all sensory input, except smell, passes through it on the way to the cortex.

fields.front: Which small structure runs hunger, thirst, body temperature, and links the nervous and hormonal systems?
fields.extra: It keeps the body in balance (homeostasis) and controls the pituitary gland just below it.

fields.front: Which structure is essential for forming new long-term memories?
fields.extra: Named for its seahorse shape; it is among the first regions damaged in Alzheimer's disease.

fields.front: Which almond-shaped structure processes fear and emotional memory?
fields.extra: Part of the limbic system and central to the fight-or-flight response.

fields.front: What thick band of fibers connects the brain's two hemispheres?
fields.extra: About 200 million nerve fibers. Cutting it to treat severe epilepsy produces a 'split brain'.

fields.front: Which side of the body does each half of the brain control?
fields.extra: The left hemisphere controls the right side of the body, and the right hemisphere controls the left.

fields.front: What do the basal ganglia help regulate?
fields.back: Smooth voluntary movement and habit learning
fields.extra: Their breakdown underlies the tremors of Parkinson's disease and the movements of Huntington's disease.

fields.front: What are the three protective membranes around the brain, from outside in?
fields.back: Dura mater, arachnoid mater, and pia mater
fields.extra: They run from the tough outer 'dura' to the delicate 'pia' hugging the brain. Bleeding between them causes subdural and subarachnoid hemorrhages.

fields.front: What clear fluid cushions the brain and fills its inner cavities?
fields.extra: Made by the choroid plexus; the brain floats in it, which drastically cuts its effective weight.

fields.front: What are the fluid-filled cavities inside the brain called?
fields.extra: There are four; they make and circulate cerebrospinal fluid.

fields.front: What barrier shields the brain by tightly filtering what crosses from the blood?
fields.extra: Formed by tightly sealed cells lining brain capillaries; it keeps out many toxins and germs, and also many drugs.

fields.front: What is the difference between gray matter and white matter?
fields.back: Gray matter is nerve cell bodies; white matter is their myelin-coated wiring
fields.extra: Gray matter does the processing; white matter connects regions. Myelin gives white matter its pale color.

fields.front: Which pea-sized gland is called the body's 'master gland'?
fields.extra: It hangs below the hypothalamus and directs many of the body's other hormone glands.

fields.front: Which tiny gland makes melatonin and helps set the sleep-wake cycle?
fields.extra: Deep in the center of the brain; Descartes called it the 'seat of the soul'.

fields.front: About how many neurons does the human brain contain?
fields.extra: Each can link to thousands of others, forming trillions of connections.

fields.front: About how much does the adult human brain weigh?
fields.extra: It is roughly 2% of body weight but uses around 20% of the body's energy.

fields.front: Why is the surface of the brain so wrinkled?
fields.back: The folds pack far more cortex into the skull
fields.extra: Spread flat, the cortex would cover roughly two square feet. The ridges are gyri and the grooves are sulci.
